planting.note: Plant fresh seeds immediately upon collection in spring (viability is very short — plant within days) in moist to wet, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Drummond red maple is the Gulf Coastal Plain variant of red maple, adapted to swamps, bottomlands, and wet flatwoods of the lower South.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic, fertile to average soil in full sun to partial shade; Drummond red maple is the variety of red maple (Acer rubrum) native to wet bottomlands, swamp forests, and pond edges of the Gulf Coastal Plain and lower Mississippi River valley. It differs from the typical red maple in larger leaves with more prominent, whitish-tomentose (densely hairy) undersides and in greater adaptation to the hot, humid, wet conditions of the Gulf South. The brilliant red fall color persists even in zone 9, providing some of the best native fall color available in the Gulf South where fall color from other trees is limited. Early red flowers in late winter announce the approach of spring. Unlike typical red maple, Drummond red maple tolerates the periodic flooding and summer heat of Gulf South bottomlands reliably. An outstanding native shade tree for wet, moist, or low-lying areas in zones 6–9.

planting.note: Plant seeds in fall after cold stratification (or plant naturally in fall for natural stratification) in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; bigleaf maple grows naturally in moist, coastal forests and stream banks of the Pacific Northwest and is the largest maple in western North America.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; bigleaf maple is the dominant large deciduous tree of moist Pacific Northwest forests, producing the largest leaves of any North American maple — up to 12 inches wide with 5 deep lobes. The massive leaves produce an extraordinary large-textured canopy unlike any other temperate tree. In Pacific Northwest old-growth forests, bigleaf maple trunks and limbs are often draped in thick carpets of mosses, ferns, and licorice fern, creating some of the most magical, fairy-tale forest scenes in North America. Long, pendulous clusters of small, fragrant yellow-green flowers attract early-season pollinators. The paired samaras are large, forming a unique, U-shaped pair. Fall color is golden-yellow to bright orange. The sap can be processed into maple syrup, though production is lower than sugar maple.

planting.note: Plant seeds in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky, calcareous soil in full sun to partial shade; canyon maple is adapted to the thin, rocky, limestone soils and canyon walls of the Balcones Escarpment in Texas and of canyons throughout the central Rocky Mountains.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky, calcareous or sandstone soil in partial shade in the canyon microclimate; canyon maple is the native maple of the Rocky Mountain and Great Basin canyons and the Edwards Plateau of Texas, producing spectacular fall color — brilliant orange, red, and scarlet tones — in otherwise largely dry, autumn-green landscapes. It is the maple that colors the walls of Lost Maples State Natural Area in the Texas Hill Country each October in brilliant red and orange, one of the most spectacular fall color displays in Texas. The tree grows naturally in sheltered canyon walls and north-facing slopes where moisture and shade moderate summer heat, and it is adapted to calcareous, rocky soils unlike the acidic-soil preferences of eastern maples. The small stature (10–30 feet) and excellent fall color make it the ideal native shade tree for small alkaline gardens in central Texas.

planting.note: Plant seeds in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun to partial shade; Caddo maple is a heat and drought tolerant selection of sugar maple from the Wichita Mountains of Oklahoma, significantly more adaptable to the conditions of the southern plains than northeastern sugar maple provenances.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile, deep soil in full sun to partial shade; Caddo maple is a naturally heat-tolerant and drought-adapted selection of sugar maple (Acer saccharum) collected from the Caddo-Kiowa country of the Wichita Mountains in southwestern Oklahoma — the southern edge of the species' native range. Plants grown from seed collected at this southerly provenance are significantly better adapted to the summer heat and periodic drought of the southern plains and lower Midwest than sugar maples sourced from Pennsylvania or Vermont nurseries. The fall color is the brilliant red, orange, and yellow typical of sugar maple. Used in street tree trials in Kansas and Oklahoma where its heat tolerance has proven superior to northern-sourced maples. The best available choice when a true sugar maple is desired for gardens in zones 6–8 in the transition-zone South.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; spiny black olive grows naturally in tropical coastal hammocks and the edges of mangrove in south Florida and the Caribbean, tolerating salt spray, thin alkaline soils, and drought.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy, coastal soil in full sun; spiny black olive is a native tree of tropical coastal hammocks and scrub of south Florida and the Caribbean, not related to the olive (Olea) but named for the dark, fleshy, olive-like fruits. The tree has an attractive, fine-textured, weeping canopy with long, drooping branchlets bearing small, leathery, elongated leaves and (on young growth) short, stiff spines that have given it one common name. The small fruits are eaten by tropical birds. Well adapted to coastal conditions including salt spray, rocky limestone soil, and drought — all characteristic of the Keys habitat. Widely used as an ornamental and windbreak tree in South Florida coastal landscapes. A smaller relative of the commonly planted (and non-native) black olive (Bucida buceras), which is the dominant street tree of Miami.
